request.question: What was Debbie Rowe's husband dubbed?

title: Jackson v. Jackson
Lin Huiyin
Michael Jackson
Debbie Rowe
Debbie Purdy
Buy Me
Snarling Beasties
Gallery of Fear
List of The Millers episodes
Paris Jackson (actress)
sentences: Deborah Rowe–Jackson v. Michael Joseph Jackson settled the divorce of Michael Jackson and his second wife Debbie Rowe.
Full custody was granted to Jackson.
Rowe is allowed visitation rights.
She gets property in Palm Beach, Van Nuys and Miami.
Rowe alleged sexual abuse as mentioned in the last amendment in the divorce.
Lin Huiyin (; known as Phyllis Lin or Lin Whei-yin when in the United States; 10 June 1904 – 1 April 1955) was a noted 20th-century Chinese architect and writer.

She is known to be the first female architect in modern China and her husband the famed "Father of Modern Chinese Architecture" Liang Sicheng, both of whom worked as founders and faculty in the newly formed Architecture Department of Northeastern University in 1928 and, after 1949, as professors in Tsinghua University in Beijing.
Liang and Lin began restoration work on cultural heritage sites of China in the post-imperial Republican Era of China; a passion which she would pursue to the end of her life.
The American artist Maya Lin is her niece.
<ref name = "Rowe/Kuan">Peter G. Rowe, Seng Kuan, "Architectural Encounters With Essence and Form in Modern China", MIT Press, 2002, p.219, ISBN  </ref>
Michael Joseph Jackson (August 29, 1958 – June 25, 2009) was an American singer, songwriter, and dancer.
Dubbed the "King of Pop", he was one of the most popular entertainers in the world, and was the best-selling music artist at the time of his death.
Jackson's contributions to music, dance, and fashion along with his publicized personal life made him a global figure in popular culture for over four decades.
Deborah Jeanne Rowe (born December 6, 1958) is an American nurse known for her marriage to Michael Jackson, with whom she had two children.
She lives in Palmdale, California.

Debbie Purdy (4 May 1963 – 23 December 2014) was a British music journalist and political activist from Bradford, West Yorkshire, with primary progressive multiple sclerosis, notable for her challenge to the law in England and Wales as relates to assisted suicide.
On 20 September 2009, it was announced that guidelines on assisted suicide law would be published by the UK Government.

The guidelines for England and Wales "come after a legal battle won by Debbie Purdy", as "Law Lords accepted earlier this year that [Purdy] had a right to know whether her husband would be prosecuted if he helped her to travel abroad to commit suicide."
Buy Me is a television program that has aired on HGTV in the U.S. since 2005, and on HGTV Canada since 2003, where it is that cable network's most popular show.
It is also seen in Belgium and South Africa, either dubbed or with subtitles.
It is produced by Whalley-Abbey Media Holdings (WHAM), which is owned by Debbie Travis and her husband, and produced "Debbie Travis’ Facelift".
The Snarling Beasties were formed in 1986 by Debbie Isitt and Mark Kilmurry and were influenced by the theatre of Steven Berkoff.
After performing "East" by Berkoff, Isitt became the theatre company's writer and director.
Mark Kilmurry played the lead parts in her first plays, "Gangsters" (1988), "Punch & Judy", (1989), "Femme Fatal" (1990) and "The Woman Who Cooked Her Husband" (1992).
The theatre company was very successful and won many awards including the Time Out theatre award, The Perrier Pick of the Fringe (three times) and The Independent Theatre Award.
Their theatre mixed a cinematic style with total theatre and created physical, dynamic and naturalistic plays.
The company was very influential but disbanded in 1996 though Debbie and Mark are both very successful in their chosen creative roles and still plan to do more work in the future.
Gallery of Fear (2013) is a four-part horror anthology, directed by Alan Rowe Kelly and Anthony G. Summer.
Kelly also wrote the screenplay for the film based on a story by Doug Smith.
The film stars Debbie Rochon, Raine Brown, and Susan Adriensen.
The four segments of the anthology are titled "Critics Choice," "By Her Hand, She Draws You Down," "Down the Drain," and "A Far Cry from Home."
The film was distributed by Southpaw Pictures and Tiny Core Pictures.
"The Millers" is an American sitcom that was created by Greg Garcia.
The multi-camera series premiered on CBS as part of the 2013–14 American television season and aired from October 3, 2013 to July 18, 2015.
"The Millers" is set in Leesburg, Virginia where Nathan Miller is a local television news reporter.
His sister Debbie runs a combination yoga studio/vegan restaurant with her husband Adam, with whom she has a daughter named Mikayla.
Nathan does not have any children and often hangs out with Ray, his friend who also serves as a cameraman for the fictional television station at which Nathan works.
As the series begins, Nathan and Debbie's bickering parents, Tom and Carol Miller, are returning from Myrtle Beach.
Nathan informs them he divorced his wife, Janice, several months ago.
Tom responds by deciding to leave Carol after being married for forty-three years.
Tom moves in with Debbie and Adam while Carol moves in with Nathan, each driving their children crazy.
On March 13, 2014, "The Millers" was renewed for a second season, which premiered on October 20, 2014.
Paris-Michael Katherine Jackson (born April 3, 1998) is an American actress, activist, and model.
She is the second child and only daughter of Michael Jackson and Debbie Rowe.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What was Debbie Rowe's husband dubbed?
hop1_docs: Buy Me | Buy Me is a television program that has aired on HGTV in the U.S. since 2005, and on HGTV Canada since 2003, where it is that cable network's most popular show.  It is also seen in Belgium and South Africa, either dubbed or with subtitles.  It is produced by Whalley-Abbey Media Holdings (WHAM), which is owned by Debbie Travis and her husband, and produced "Debbie Travis’ Facelift".

Debbie Burton | Debbie Burton was an American singer.  She is best known for dubbing the singing voice of the young Baby Jane Hudson (played by child actress Julie Allred) in the 1962 film "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? ", singing the song "I've Written a Letter to Daddy".  Burton also sang a duet with Bette Davis, the rock and roll song "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?" , written by Frank DeVol and Lukas Heller.  It was released as a promotional single, with Burton's rendition of "I've Written a Letter to Daddy" on the flipside.  An instrumental version of "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?"  can be heard in the movie.

Betty Noyes | Elizabeth Noyes Hand (October 11, 1912 – December 24, 1987) was a singer and actress best known for dubbing two of Debbie Reynolds' numbers in the 1952 film "Singin' in the Rain."  Today, this is a well-known example of dubbing in a movie musical; ironically, Reynolds's character in "Singin' in the Rain" was supposedly dubbing for another character.

Jackson v. Jackson | Deborah Rowe–Jackson v. Michael Joseph Jackson settled the divorce of Michael Jackson and his second wife Debbie Rowe.  Full custody was granted to Jackson.  Rowe is allowed visitation rights.  She gets property in Palm Beach, Van Nuys and Miami.  Rowe alleged sexual abuse as mentioned in the last amendment in the divorce.
Debbie Rowe | Deborah Jeanne Rowe (born December 6, 1958) is an American nurse known for her marriage to Michael Jackson, with whom she had two children.  She lives in Palmdale, California.

Debbie Boyd | Debbie Boyd (born November, 1957 in Gainesville, Florida) is a Representative in the Florida House of Representatives.  She received her associate degree in building construction from Santa Fe Community College in 1990.  She resides in Newberry, Florida and lives with her husband Tommy Boyd.

Debbi Fields | Debbi Fields (born September 18, 1956) is the founder and spokesperson of Mrs. Fields Bakeries.  Additionally, she has written several cookbooks and makes cookies.  She currently lives in Nashville, Tennessee, with her husband, former Holiday Inn and Harrah's CEO Michael Rose.
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passages state Debbie Rowe is "known for her marriage to Michael Jackson" (Deborah Jeanne Rowe). Bridge entity: Michael Jackson (also cited as Michael Joseph Jackson). Missing fact: the specific nickname/epithet he was "dubbed" (e.g., which title) is not present in the passages. Retrieval directions: open Michael Jackson’s main biography (e.g., his Wikipedia page or an authoritative bio) and search for "dubbed," "nicknamed," or phrases like "dubbed the" to identify the epithet; alternatively query "Michael Jackson dubbed OR 'dubbed the' site:wikipedia.org". Clarification: identity is unambiguous in the passages (Michael Jackson).
response.hop2_query: Michael Jackson dubbed "King of Pop" site:wikipedia.org OR "dubbed the" "Michael Jackson"
hop2_docs: Michael Jackson | Michael Joseph Jackson (August 29, 1958 – June 25, 2009) was an American singer, songwriter, and dancer.  Dubbed the "King of Pop", he was one of the most popular entertainers in the world, and was the best-selling music artist at the time of his death.  Jackson's contributions to music, dance, and fashion along with his publicized personal life made him a global figure in popular culture for over four decades.

The Original Soul of Michael Jackson | The Original Soul of Michael Jackson is a remix album of songs by Michael Jackson, recorded and published early in his solo career in the early 1970s and remixed in 1987.  While the album claims to 'contains music never before released', the only new song available was a re-dubbed cover of Edwin Starr's 1970 hit "Twenty-Five Miles", sung by the Jackson Five and solely credited to Michael.  The original recording of the song was included on the 2009 set "".  Some songs were newly edited on the album, with a newly dubbed version of "Dancing Machine". " Ain't No Sunshine" were also slightly different in the vocals and a modern drum machine were added to "Twenty-Five Miles".  "Melodie" was planned for a single in an effort to promote the record in the US, but was scrapped for a promo-only single "Twenty-Five Miles"/"Up On The Housetop".

List of artists influenced by Michael Jackson | Michael Jackson, also known as the King of Pop, was a pop, music, dance and fashion icon.  His distinctive sound and style has influenced numerous artists of various music genres.

One More Chance (Michael Jackson song) | "One More Chance" is a song by American recording artist Michael Jackson.  It was penned by R. Kelly and co-produced by Jackson and Kelly for inclusion on Jackson's compilation album "Number Ones", released in 2003.  The song was later included on the Australian, French, Swedish and Indonesian editions of "King of Pop" and the Japanese limited edition of "The Ultimate Collection" box set.

King of Pop (album) | King of Pop is a compilation album by American recording artist Michael Jackson released in commemoration of Michael Jackson's 50th birthday.  The album differs significantly in each country it was released because fan voting was employed to determine the songs for each country's version.  The album's title comes from the title Jackson acquired approximately 20 years earlier.  The album's launch was made public on June 20, 2008, with the official announcement of the Australian version.  The first release came with the German edition (which is identical to the Swiss edition) on August 22, 2008.

Unity: The Latin Tribute to Michael Jackson | Unity: The Latin Tribute to Michael Jackson, also known as The Unity Project, is a collaborative musical project aimed at creating a Michael Jackson tribute album inspired by Latin music styles.  The leader of the project is Peruvian-American musician Tony Succar and vocals are provided by a number of leading artists in Latin music genres, especially Salsa.  Succar's aim with the project was to celebrate Jackson's music and legacy by combining it with a Latin-orchestrated sound.  The stated goal is "to honor the legacy of Michael Jackson, the King of Pop.  By remaining true to his artistic essence, excellence, and music we seek to unify all human beings."  Upon its release, the album debuted at number one on the "Billboard" Tropical Albums chart.

Michael Jackson: The Last Photo Shoot | Michael Jackson: The Last Photo Shoot is an upcoming documentary film directed by Craig J. Williams.  This historical documentary about Michael Jackson's last magazine cover-shoots in 2007.  The film is a unique story told through the eyes of Michael's closest friends, photographers, and stylists that had helped Jackson prepare for his 2007 United States comeback after several years of living in seclusion overseas.  In September of that year, photographer Bruce Weber and "Vogue" fashion editor as well as Jackson's personal stylist Rushka Bergman captured the magic of the King of Pop for the 25th anniversary reissue of his hit album "Thriller", entitled "Thriller 25".  Later that month, Jackson did another shoot and an interview for "Ebony" magazine's December 2007 issue.  This would mark Jackson's first United States interview and magazine story in over a decade.
hop2_titles: The Original Soul of Michael Jackson
List of artists influenced by Michael Jackson
Unity: The Latin Tribute to Michael Jackson
Michael Jackson: The Last Photo Shoot
response.summary_2: Debbie Rowe's husband, Michael Jackson, was dubbed the "King of Pop." Michael Joseph Jackson (1958–2009) was an American singer, songwriter, and dancer widely known by that epithet.
